questions.question: According to research, what neurobiological changes occur when you engage in genuine prosocial behavior?

options.text: No measurable neurobiological changes occur
Reward system activation, dopamine release, oxytocin system activation, reduced cortisol, and lower inflammation
Only cortisol reduction, no reward activation
These changes only occur in trained meditation practitioners

questions.explanation: Research shows helping activates multiple neurobiological systems: the ventral striatum and reward pathways (creating 'helper's high'), dopamine release (pleasure and motivation), oxytocin system (bonding and social affiliation), reduced cortisol (stress hormone), and lower inflammation markers. This is the neurobiology of altruism—helping literally activates the same reward circuits as food or money. However, these benefits require the right conditions: freely chosen helping within your capacity with genuine motivation. Obligatory helping under stress may actually increase cortisol and inflammation instead.

questions.question: What is Loving-Kindness Meditation (LKM) and what does research evidence show about its effects?

options.text: A visualization practice with no empirical support
A contemplative practice cultivating warmth and care; RCT evidence shows increases in positive emotions, social connection, self-compassion, and modest reductions in depression/anxiety
A relaxation technique unrelated to compassion
Only effective for Buddhist practitioners

questions.explanation: Loving-Kindness Meditation (LKM) is a practice where you silently repeat phrases like 'May I be happy, healthy, safe...' directed toward yourself, loved ones, neutral people, difficult people, and all beings. RCT evidence (MODERATE quality) shows LKM increases positive emotions, life satisfaction, social connection, and self-compassion, and modestly reduces depression and anxiety in some studies. It also enhances vagal tone (parasympathetic activation). The practice works through repetition over weeks—it's about cultivating the intention of care, not forcing warm feelings in every session. Evidence grade is MODERATE for wellbeing, EMERGING for clinical populations.

questions.question: What did the Dunn, Aknin, & Norton (2008) study on prosocial spending find?

options.text: Spending money on yourself increases happiness more than giving it away
Prosocial spending (giving money to others) produced significantly greater happiness than personal spending
How you spend money has no effect on happiness
Only wealthy people benefit from prosocial spending

questions.explanation: The foundational study randomly assigned participants to spend money on themselves (personal spending) or others (prosocial spending) and found prosocial spending produced significantly greater happiness increases—even controlling for amount. This isn't just correlation ('happy people give more'); it's experimental evidence that the act of giving itself increases happiness. The effect has been replicated cross-culturally (wealthy and low-income nations, individualist and collectivist cultures) and even in toddlers. Critical moderators: autonomy (freely chosen), connection (creates social connection), and impact (you can see the effect). These are the same moderators from Lesson 2.

questions.question: From an evolutionary perspective, why do humans engage in altruistic behavior toward non-kin?

options.text: Evolution doesn't explain altruism; it's purely cultural
Reciprocal altruism (future reciprocation), indirect reciprocity (reputation), and group selection mechanisms
Humans are the only species that helps non-kin
All helping is secretly selfish gene propagation

questions.explanation: Evolutionary explanations for non-kin altruism include: (1) Reciprocal altruism—'I help you today, you help me tomorrow' (requires repeated interactions and memory); (2) Indirect reciprocity—others observe your helping and are more likely to help you (reputation building); (3) Group selection—groups with more altruistic members may outcompete selfish groups; (4) Cultural evolution—prosocial norms transmitted faster than genes. These explain population-level patterns over millennia but do NOT mean your individual helping is purely selfish calculation. Humans have capacity for genuine altruism motivated by empathy and values. Evolution creates the capacity; you choose how to express it.

questions.question: When might Loving-Kindness Meditation NOT be appropriate or safe?

options.text: LKM is safe and appropriate for everyone in all circumstances
For trauma survivors (can activate trauma responses), those with severe anhedonia or dissociation, or when the practice is intensely distressing
Only if you don't believe in meditation
LKM is never appropriate; it's pseudoscience

questions.explanation: While LKM is generally safe and has RCT evidence, it's not appropriate for everyone in all circumstances. Trauma survivors may find that directing compassion toward themselves activates trauma responses rather than soothing them—they may need trauma-informed therapy first or trauma-sensitive meditation instruction. People with severe anhedonia (inability to feel warmth or pleasure) may struggle to generate any compassion and feel shame at 'failing' the practice. If LKM consistently feels harmful rather than neutral or helpful, discontinue and consult a mental health professional. This is the same clinical limitation pattern we've seen: evidence-based practices still require matching the intervention to the person's current state.
